then the pipeline does research, drafting, application of the writing guidelines, of course, polishing, fact-checking.

So the reason I want to give you that, some context about how we work, because we generally need the content managers to be, like the word manager is in the title, not because you're managing people, you are managing a pipeline, right?

I have to understand the inputs that go in, the idea of what it's doing, you know, the rough kind of like outline, you don't have to be an engineer, you don't have to, that we have an engineering team for that.

I need you to do this.

What I'm wondering is that, let's say there's a new client I'm supposed to create content for, and does this client come already with their goals and their expectations and what they want, or are we supposed now, am I supposed as a manager to help them realize this, let's say they want blog articles and, yeah, blog on the client side, you mean?
Yeah, yeah, sure.
Oh, yeah, yeah.
The content managers don't interact directly with clients a whole lot, so you don't have to worry about it too much.

Every once in a while, maybe you will interact with a client, especially if there's like a specific deliverable or you're presenting something in a meeting, but in general, the client-facing role is the managing editor, and so you would be working with the managing editor.
They would be running the meetings and doing the client interviews and all that, but as a content manager, your roles consist of doing keyword research and therefore being able to hack.
Give feedback and say, hey, you know, I know they want these topics, but these keywords are like really tough or, you know, we, I don't know if this is going to deliver the goals that they want.
So it's not like you're blindly executing.
You definitely can tactics with your ME and, and they can, you know, we can contextualize it because at the end of the day, we are the experts.
They are not.
Most of our clients do not have like a full-time content operation.
That's why they hired us.
Or even if they do have a content operation, they may not have somebody full-time on it.
And so that, the idea that they would know exactly what they want and really be able to like speak with authority about keyword strategies and all that, usually not.
So you definitely are participating in that, but you do not have to worry necessarily about doing a lot of client-facing presentations or things like that.
You are working largely internally.

And so you're able to see the gaps.
And when I say gap, the way I think about it personally, because I do manage clients as well, right?
I have three clients that I work with in addition to what else I'm doing.
And the reason I work with those clients is so that I get that view, so that I understand how our tools are actually working or not.
Honestly, not like what I wish they would be, right?
But what they actually are doing.
And so the CM is that interface where you're going like, hey, this is what we say the pipeline is supposed to do.
And this is what I actually have to deliver to the client.
And I've got this gap.
And that gap can be time.
It can be effort.
It can be, like, just messy middle stuff.
Like, I have to do a bunch of things to get it to ship, to shippable shape.
And as I mentioned before, our ideal is like, hey, a handful of minutes per piece should be able to get you delivery for the client.
But sometimes the delta is big because what we'll do is like, let's say it's a brand new client.
We'll bring them in.
We do the sales call.
They agree.
They set the contract.
Then we bring them in.
And then we do a kickoff call and several follow-up calls in what we call a sprint, right?
A project sprint or a client sprint.
And that sprint is like eight to 12 weeks when they first join or when they first sign up so that we can understand them.
Build those core artifacts that I talked about.
Build the pipelines needed to deliver the client work, whether that's programmatic SEO where we're building a bunch of pages or it's just blog posts or whatever it is, right?
And then we operate, hopefully operate that machinery enough to calibrate the content where they're like, oh, cool, this is getting really close.
Okay.
And then we hand it off to us.
Steady state client group.
Now, if you're working in the sprint group, which, you know, I don't know, you know, we did, we'd rotate people in and out of it because it's helpful sometimes to understand that.
But it's not, it's not like every week you're with a different client.
We generally try to focus CMs on a handful of clients so that they can get to know them and understand them.
If you're working in the sprint group, the answer to your question is like very involved because you're trying to like help develop the pipeline.
You do not have to engineer it.
We have engineers for that.
But a feedback, like really close loop feedback on how it's operating, all of that stuff.
When you're in a steady state, you know, out of sprint team where you're operating that client's deliverables every week, your feedback is really all about efficiency.
It's like, hey, you know, I've got five pieces to deliver for this client and it's taking me like an hour and a half to get each one done.
Why is that?
And by done, I mean like, okay, I ran the pipelines.
ran like five or six pipelines.
Now it's taking me like...
Like an hour and a half to like edit these or get them in shape.
And then the job or the, frankly, like the effort that you take to make your life easier is what do I need to do between when it exits the pipeline and deliver for the client?
Let me document that, whether that's in a cloud project or a chattypt project or just documenting very carefully what you have to do.
And then understanding the commonalities.
Because there's going to be one-off stuff in every article.
That's why you're here, right?
There's going to be like weird stuff that happens.
Okay, you know, you edit it and you move on with your day.
But if it's something where you're like, hey, this weird thing has happened five times this week.
Okay, let's figure out how to fix that, right?
Did you have to like remove a certain section five times this week?
Well, let's put an instruction into the pipeline.
like, don't do this section, please.
Thank you.

We don't want it.
If the client doesn't like it, please do.
Do not do it.

And so whether that's an update to an artifact or whether it's a tweak to the actual pipeline, you are coming to an understanding of what you need to close that gap, to make your life easier, to make you more efficient, to make you faster.
And as far as the pipelines go, that's really your interaction with it.
You are managing them.
And then when you see an opportunity for improvement, you have the ability to document it, file a ticket, and go like, okay, cool.
Hopefully, they'll get to that in the next cycle, and then my life will get easier, right?
And if you do that enough, it makes your job really efficient and a pleasure because you're able to run the pipelines, do a handful of edits to each piece, and then get your client delivery out.
And that work, you have to be, just be honest, you have to be excited about that work because it is part of the job.
